speaker: interviewee
text: 4-- Barbara Drotow, survivor, interviewer, Mary Rothschild. It's the second tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I guess the baby got sick. And he probably was dying from starvation. And we didn't know, you know. And, and also, I guess, there was a shock to his system-- all of a sudden, no, no mother's milk. And all we had is just flour and water that we would cook it like this. And my sister came, went again to visit Mama in the hospital. And, and I was holding the baby. She told me, give him to eat. And she said, if he's hungry, don't you dare to eat yourself. You give it to him. Otherwise, he'll die. So you are, we are big. We-- somehow she said, I'm going to go. And I'll beg from the women that they should give us. And I'll do for them. I'll clean up their house and so forth. And when, and then he-- I was holding the baby. My sister's coming-- and this, I remember. You know, I-- when I was holding him, his head went like this. And I didn't know. I mean, when you are, you are close to seven, you don't know what that is. And there was no rigor mortis yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was coming. I said, Luba, Luba, fast. I was talking to her, of course, in Yiddish and in Polish. And I said, something is wrong with Leibel. And his head is falling. And, and she ran in. And I guess my sister knew. She said, [? Boronya, ?] [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH]. He died. I said, why? He said, she said, because he didn't have enough food.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in December. This I know. And she went to some people. And to some people she said, we have to bury him. And there was no rabbi there. There were just peasants. She went to some, she went to one of the neighbors. And she said, our baby died. And, and she said, we want to bury him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were Muslims so I guess that they-- you have to bury fast. And, and they asked us, did you touch the baby? And I don't know what they asked my sister. But they did not want to come help us. So maybe because it was the Muslim religion. And in the villages they were really backwards. So I guess it was a Jewish baby. Maybe it was unclean or whatever. whatever the.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so my sister started-- she said, [? Boronya, ?] you come with me. And we have to dig it out. So she borrowed-- she borrowed two shovels for the big seven-year-old one and for herself. And it was in December. In Asia Minor, it's cold. And the ground was very, very tough. And so there was another-- there was a man that saw us doing it. And the cemetery was in the middle of the, of the village. And he helped my sister. And we, my sister-- when the hole was dug, she said, we will wash the baby. Because that's what you have to do. Those things, my sister knew. We washed Leibel. And we put him-- she said she dressed him. And we put Leibel in and covered him up. And it was towards the evening. And we went back home. And she said, we are going to visit. She said, there was no Leibel to take care of. So my sister said, tomorrow we're going to visit Mama. And you don't tell her nothing. Again, she was warning me that she will pinch me. And I guess it was, it was working like a charm. And the next day, we went to-- I went. And I was holding on to her. And we walked to the [INAUDIBLE], which was about 11 kilometers. And we walked. And she, she said, go to this room. And she said, I'm going to go and get-- I don't know what she was going to get. Oh, yes. She had some food. And she was bribing even the people that working in the, in the village, in the hospital for a little bit food. You know, she was bringing them some, some things like dried fruit that were in the village that she got from the-- to bring some for mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she brought some for, for the-- especially, there was a nurse that she said, take care of my mama. I'm going to bring you something. And she said, I'm going to bring, give it to [? Yenna, ?] to the nurse. And you go in. And it was room number-- I don't know. And, but I knew. And she said-- and I walked into the room. She said, wait there for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was down the corridor. And this I remember-- you'd be surprised. When you are seven, some things-- and I wanted to see the mama, because she was almost a month in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I ran to the room. And I said, where's my mama? And the lady said some-- and I didn't see her, so I got panicky. And I ran out back. And my sister was coming towards me. And I said, Mama is not in the bed. And she said, what do you mean? So she grabbed me. And we ran. And Mama was not in the bed. Mama expired during the night. And [INAUDIBLE] it's like in two, two three days-- probably wasn't two-- we lost our-- we lost Mama and Leibel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we walked back to the village. And she said, now we have each other. And maybe Papa will live, because they took him away, too. She said, maybe he will. He went to kill Germans. And maybe he will alive. So you have you and I. She said, I heard that there is a [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH] which is a orphanage in the other village. And this is an orphanage where there are Russian children and Polish children. And she said, there are two orphanages there. But there is a good orphanage where they also give more food, because they get it from the [? UNRRA ?] which is from some-- through Poland, they were getting things-- I mean, from people that were giving from, from, from the United States and from England.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took me by, she, she took me by the hand. And she said, we're going to go to this village. There were some things that were left, so she put everything in, in, in some, some packages. And she sold a lot of things. She was smart. My sister was-- she was terrific till today. Today, she's, she's always broken down.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we got some-- she sold some of the things, and I guess also some jewelry that was still left over. She sold it and she got some money. And she said, we are going-- come. We're going to go to this, to the other village next to Andijan. And we will go to the good orphanage where they give more food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember, she came. She took me by the hand again. And we got dressed nice. And we had some and-- whatever we had. And this I remember. She came, and she asked [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH]. Where is the office? And they told her. And she walked in. And they said, what are you doing here? And we spoke Polish-- both of us. We also spoke Russian. And she said, my mother died. And my sister was only 12 and 1/2. She was still-- and she said, and we want to, we need to go to a orphanage. We don't want to live by ourselves, because we are afraid. And they took us to the orphanage. And that's where I spent the next, the years of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: When my sister-- I was three years there. And I don't know-- two or three years. And they took my sister out from the orphanage, and because she was around 14. And they sent her to-- it's a place where you have teenagers there. And you have to work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, and I begged. I said, don't take her. And she said, listen, don't leave my little sister there. We have been through thick and thin together. But they said, you have to go to this. You can visit her.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SCOFFS] Visit. And they send her about 500 or 600 kilometers from me. Also in Uzbekistan, but in a different place. And that's where she was. This was a place there where they had teenagers. And also, they were working. So and my sister was there in the [INAUDIBLE] which is, it's a school for teenagers where you work most of the time. And she was working in a place where they make silk thread. So she was standing in the water. And that's where she got her sick feet. And, but she was writing to me letters. And she said, don't-- I will be visiting you. And she was writing to-- every couple of months I would get a letter from her. And I was, meanwhile, in the orphanage. That's where I spent my time. And it wasn't too bad. I mean, we were, we used to get rations-- also the children. We got about 250 gram of bread. But we were just the same as all the other Russian children. Everybody was hungry. But we went to school. And we had a dream of going back. And my sister always, she said, we will go back to Poland, maybe somebody from the families. We did not really know how bad it was, the atrocities. And she said-- and then, of course, while we were the-- we were for four years, from 1942 till 19-- when the, let me see, 19-- when the Russians started occupying back Poland. So it's still probably 1945 or 1946. I know that we left in 1946 with the entire-- my sister said, listen, I'm going to go back to Poland. You go with your orphanage. This was because most of the children were from Poland. So I will find you. She came to me, I remember. And she said, don't forget. Don't forget. You go. We will go back to Poland. And in 1946, the entire orphanage was evacuated back to Poland. And my sister said she's going to be in-- I was miserable, because I was also afraid that I might not meet my sister. But when my sister says she'll find me, then she'll find me. And I was in Bielsko. It was near Katowice, in the orphanage. And I was waiting. Maybe my sister will find me. And sure enough, one day, my sister comes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and I said, how did you find me? She said she's in a place back in Poland. She went by herself back to Poland. She said, [? Boronya, ?] nobody was left from our family. I was right. I, nobody is left. And she was around, she was around 16, something like, or 17. And she said nobody was left. We are alone. So I said, but how did you find me? She said, I found-- there was a boy there. His name was Menache. And his mother took him out of the orphanage when they were going back to Poland. And that's how she found. She said, Menache, do you know where [? Bronka ?] is? And he said, yes. They are in Bielsko. So my sister didn't tell nobody. And she came. And-- again, it starts the smuggling [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, don't tell anybody. I want to take you out from here. We're going to go back. We're going to go to Israel if-- we will still look around. If we won't find anybody, I don't want to live in this, in this godforsaken country where they killed everybody. And we're going to go, we're going to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, but I don't want to tell anybody. Don't tell nobody here. And I will-- so my-- didn't tell anybody. And at night, the little things that I had in the orphanage, she packed it. And she said she's taking me for a walk. And we never came back. We took the bus back.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was in, and my sister was in Bielawa, which is not too far from Breslau and from [? Wroclaw. ?] These were the places where it was the Germany before, and Poland took it. And she said, I am in a kibbutz. She did tell me, I am in kibbutz. We are going to go to Eretz Yisrael. We're going to go to Israel, where at least if we have to die or something has to-- we won't. But at least we will be Jews. She said, she said, I looked around. Nobody was left. Our entire family was wiped out. And this was a family of 10 brothers. My father has 10 brothers, two sisters. As I said, my mother had-- there were four brothers and three sisters-- not even a trace from my mother's family. And I have two very, very old uncles in-- one is in Miami, and one is in New York. They are very old. And this is the only family that was left. Now, my, we stayed for a while in, in Poland. My sister took me to this place where she was. After fruitless searches, maybe some uncles or some aunts were left. Nobody was left. So in 19-- I came back to Poland in 1946. In 19-- the end, I think, of 1947, we left with the entire-- and also, we went to an orphanage that was just from Jewish children that were left wherever there were some Jewish children, or they were hiding, or they were in monasteries. But they were bright enough to come. You know, when you are a little bit-- and this was where people were finding Jewish children that knew that they are Jewish. And we went to this-- this was a kinder kibbutz, which means a kibbutz that was going to go to Israel with the surviving children. And my sister was there. And she brought me, because she was already in the kibbutz. And I loved it there. And she said, we're going to Eretz Yisrael. And so that's where we stayed. In 1948-- oh, not-- 1948, yes, the end of 19-- she said, we are going. No, in 1947, we left Poland. We went to France. And we joined with other Jewish children that were in orphanages to go to Israel. And we stayed for a half a year or a year. I don't remember exactly the years, but with other children. And then one of the uncles, by a chance, through somebody else-- that we stayed in [INAUDIBLE]. It was 60 miles under Paris. And one of our uncles found us-- the one that is still alive in New York. And when they heard that Chaim's children survived, the two girls, so they came to visit us. And he came to visit us. And he said, you have another uncle, Yitzrek, that is alive. So Uncle Nachum came to visit us. And he lived in Paris in France. But my sister said, we are going to Israel. I'm not staying in Europe. That's it. And how they survived-- so I find out that my Uncle Isaac was in Russia during the war. And that's how he survived. My Uncle Nathan, I don't know-- I think that-- I cannot-- I don't-- he looked really like a goy, like a goy, like a, like a Gentile. So he somehow survived the war. I don't remember exactly the details, you know. And meanwhile, we went with this-- they have the Jewish children, wherever, whatever children survived. And we, there was a big shipment. My sister said, we're going to go to Marseille. And we are going to go to Israel. We will go illegally. So I will let you know what kind of, what kind of name you will go under. Because the, the Haganah and the Bricha, which is under the army that was before there was the, I mean, the Israeli army. And they were smuggling out Jewish children. I was not on the Exodus, but I was on a ship that was similar to it. And she said, we are going to Israel. So we went on a ship. And I remember it. In Marseille, there were a lot of children from different places in France and in Poland, and especially in-- wherever they found some children. And we were all-- I remember that she, my sister, said, your name is going to be Miriam . [? Amis ?] And we will go. And we will go. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it, Barbara?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940-- the end of 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were then 15 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, 1948 we came to Israel. Yes. I was then. Yes, I was close to 15. We came to-- we stayed in Poland for a year. We stayed in France. And then we went to Israel. My sister went to Israel. I stayed in a kibu-- in, we decided to go on a kibbutz. I went-- my sister stayed with me for a while in the kibbutz. While I was-- going backwards-- while I was in France, I was in the orphanage. My sister was, went to a school in [INAUDIBLE]. And she said-- and we went to Israel. And then we, I, I, I was in kibbutz. I was in the kibbutz till 19-- till 1955. I went-- I went to the Israeli-- my sister was there also. But she was in the, in the city. I, I, I finished high school in Israel. I went to the Israeli Army. I love Israel. I stayed on the kibbutz, because I, I was a patriot. And my-- I was in the kibbutz. Then I was in the army. While I was in the army, by chance, I, my husband came to visit his-- he had a, he had sisters living in Israel and that survived the camps, that survived the camps. And his mother was in Israel there. So he came to visit them. And my sister didn't live too far from there T Tiv'on. And during while I was in the army in Israel, I came to visit. And we had, sort of, a blind date. We met each other in the, in the bus. And then we found out that they also-- my sister and Al's sister were neighbors. And we met each other. And we got married. I was reluctant to leave Israel, because I didn't know what's in the United States. And my husband convinced me. He lived for-- after we got married, he lived for a while in Israel with me. He said, listen, try America. If you won't like it, you can always go back. I did not want to separate from my sister. That's the only one that was left alive. And I said, I'll try it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1956, we came to New York. And I, meanwhile, I got pregnant with my older son. And we stayed in New York for a short-- for three or four years. And we then came to Los Angeles, and where I had my other son. And that's how I came, came out here.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister still lives in Israel. Her children live in Israel. One son lives here, and one is, lives in Israel. I work-- I got my schooling in Israel. I finished the army. I finished-- I matriculated in Israel. I have a-- I'm elementary school teacher by profession. And now, I work, I'm a director of a early childhood education center in a synagogue. I always worked in, in places where you instilled the Jewish spirit. With me working, it was a mission. So just to get back through the par-- sometimes through the children, the parents that are away so much from Judaism are really coming back to it. After a million and a half children, there are not too many. There were a lot of Einsteins, and Modiglianis and Chagalls went up in smoke. And so for me, it's a mission to work with the young children. I wound up here. I have two children. My older one is Harry. Harry is very much involved in the second generation. He is a scholar in the studies of the Holocaust. As a matter of fact, he writes handbooks and guides for, for docents, that he writes guides for them and handbooks and how to train them. He works also, he volunteered for years in the Museums of the Holocaust at the Martyrs Memorial. And we are involved in perpetuating Judaism. And when I especially, when I work with young children, I feel that Hitler did not accomplish what he set out to do, to wipe us off the face of the Earth. That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you like to show us some pictures now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The picture on the left is a picture of my mama. My mother's name was Mina Kenig, from the home [INAUDIBLE]. I guess this picture has been taken in the late '20s. This is one picture, the only, that you can see it has a lot of lines there. My sister brought it to me two years ago. And she made a copy. That's the only copy that from the entire family. Nobody-- this was made in [POLISH], Poland. Nobody from the people from [POLISH], she says survived. The picture on the right, this is my Papa, [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH], Chaim Kenig. He, the only two brothers that were left, which I mentioned already, Yitzrek and Nachum. And this is Chaim Kenig. It has been taken in Kutno, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been also in the '20s, because my father was born at the turn of the century. So I don't know. He must-- maybe he would be now in his late 80s, probably. This is a picture of my sister, Luba, and myself. This picture was made in Paris when we went on the way to Israel. And we made a picture. This was in 1948-- the end of, probably, 1948. This is a picture of myself in Los Angeles here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Barbara Drotow, [? Bronka ?] Kenig from home. And I don't know. It's quite a while. I don't look the same when I was a young mommy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately in the late '70s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles. This is a picture of-- the one on the right is my husband. And I think this is when he came to the United States in 1949. And the picture on the left is my picture. It was taken in 1955 while I was still in the army. And that's when I met my husband. I was a sergeant in the Israeli Army. This is a picture of my family, my husband and I, and Harry, and Bobby. And this picture was taken in 1963 I think, or '64.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles.
